paper_title: DragMesh-2: Physically Plausible Dexterous Hand-Object Interaction with Articulated Objects

direction_board.description: Add wrist force-torque sensing or tactile fingertips to the PICA pipeline. Compare the same architecture with vs. without force feedback to establish whether PICA's proxies are a substitute or complement for direct force sensing. The paper identifies action saturation as a key failure mode under strong damping — force feedback would allow the policy to regulate grip force directly rather than push to actuator boundaries.
direction_board.rationale: The paper identifies this as the primary limitation. Without this experiment, the 'robustness without force feedback' claim is only validated in simulation. A real robot experiment with force-torque sensing would be the most impactful immediate follow-up.

minimum_viable_experiment.setup: Add 6-axis F/T sensor at wrist to same Isaac Gym simulation. Compare PICA (no force) vs PICA+force (force in observation) vs PICA+force+force-reward across ×1, ×2, ×4 damping.
minimum_viable_experiment.expected_outcome: Force feedback should improve ×4 success by reducing action saturation (clip099). If PICA+force matches or exceeds PICA at ×4 without increased clip099, force feedback is a substitute. If PICA+force significantly exceeds PICA's ceiling, they are complementary.
minimum_viable_experiment.key_metric: ×4 deterministic success, clip099 at ×4, detach_proxy at ×4

direction_board.description: Validate the PICA-trained policy on a physical robotic hand (e.g., Allegro Hand, LEAP Hand, or Shadow Hand) interacting with real articulated objects (cabinet doors, drawers). Introduce real damping variation through lubrication, surface materials, or handle modifications.
direction_board.rationale: The paper has zero real-world quantitative validation. The hardware example is purely qualitative. This is the most important credibility gap. Real-world contact loads differ from simulated damping in important ways (friction anisotropy, stiction, non-linear compliance).

minimum_viable_experiment.setup: Train PICA in simulation on a single cabinet door. Deploy zero-shot on physical hardware with the same object. Vary surface friction (taped handle, lubricated hinge) and measure success rate over 10+ trials per condition.
minimum_viable_experiment.expected_outcome: Some sim-to-real drop is expected. The question is whether PICA's robustness to damping variation in sim transfers to physical friction/hinge variation in reality. A success rate >50% at the hardest condition would be promising.
minimum_viable_experiment.key_metric: Sim-to-real success rate, action saturation (joint commands), contact maintenance duration

direction_board.description: Extend evaluation beyond damping to include friction coefficient variation, surface compliance, handle geometry perturbation, joint stiction/jamming, and manufacturing tolerances. Current work varies only one dynamics parameter.
direction_board.rationale: Real-world robustness requires handling multi-dimensional variation. A ×4 damping success of 0.56 may not generalize to ×1 damping + 2× friction + worn hinge. This is the most important generalization question after sim-to-real.

minimum_viable_experiment.setup: Define a 4-dimensional contact-load space (damping ∈ [×1,×4], friction ∈ [0.3, 1.0], compliance ∈ [stiff, soft], stiction ∈ [none, some]). Train PICA on all four dims simultaneously. Evaluate held-out combinations.
minimum_viable_experiment.expected_outcome: PICA should show better generalization across multiple dims than flat-history PPO. The GLA encoder should encode multi-dimensional contact state. Key finding: which dimensions interact?
minimum_viable_experiment.key_metric: Success rate at each held-out combination, action saturation, contact failure modes

direction_board.description: Integrate the PICA-trained hand policy with a whole-body humanoid controller. Use the heuristic dataset as a motion-scale prior for coordinated locomotion + articulated-object interaction (e.g., walking to a cabinet, opening it, retrieving an object).
direction_board.rationale: The paper identifies this as a concrete next step. The dataset contains full hand–object motion clips extending beyond the hand alone. Floating-hand manipulation is a significant abstraction from real humanoid use cases.

minimum_viable_experiment.setup: Mount the PICA hand controller on a simulated humanoid (e.g., H1 or Digit). Add a whole-body controller (e.g., Model Predictive Control for locomotion). Task: walk to cabinet, open door to 90°, walk away. Compare floating-hand baseline vs whole-body integration.
minimum_viable_experiment.expected_outcome: Whole-body coupling should succeed but with higher failure rate than floating hand (balance constraints). The key insight is whether PICA's contact robustness survives when the hand reference frame moves.
minimum_viable_experiment.key_metric: Task success, balance maintenance, hand–object contact duration, whole-body coordination cost

direction_board.description: Replace position-increment action with torque or impedance control. The paper identifies position-increment action saturation as a key failure mode. Torque control would naturally avoid the 'push to joint limits' problem.
direction_board.rationale: The position-increment interface is a design choice that creates the saturation failure mode. Torque control may inherently produce more robust contact through passive compliance. This would test whether PICA's contributions are interface-independent.

minimum_viable_experiment.setup: Reimplement PICA with torque-control action space (joint torques instead of PD position increments). Compare success rates and action distribution at ×1 and ×4 damping. Check if torque-based PICA achieves higher success or lower clip099 at ×4.
minimum_viable_experiment.expected_outcome: Torque control should reduce action saturation naturally. PICA's auxiliary signals may be less necessary with torque control, or they may still be needed for contact-proxy information. This disentangles PICA's contributions.
minimum_viable_experiment.key_metric: ×4 success, action magnitude distribution, contact maintenance duration

direction_board.description: Train PICA on a subset of GAPartNet objects and test zero-shot on held-out categories (e.g., train on StorageFurniture, test on Dishwasher). Current evaluation is per-object but all objects are seen during training.
direction_board.rationale: Generalization to unseen articulated objects is the practical requirement. Handle geometry, mass distribution, and joint range vary significantly. The paper's dataset is heavily dominated by StorageFurniture (256/277).

minimum_viable_experiment.setup: Leave-one-category-out cross-validation. Train on 6 categories, test on the held-out 7th. Measure success across ×1, ×2, ×4 damping. Compare PICA vs state-only PPO vs GRU-PPO for zero-shot generalization.
minimum_viable_experiment.expected_outcome: Significant drop expected, especially on geometrically different categories. PICA's contact-proxy signals should generalize better than task-progress-only baselines because contact maintenance is geometry-invariant while optimal progress is geometry-dependent.
minimum_viable_experiment.key_metric: Zero-shot success rate, contact-proxy diagnostics, per-category breakdown

direction_board.direction: Make clip099 a Loss Term, Not Just a Diagnostic
direction_board.description: Currently clip099 is reported as a diagnostic but not directly minimized. Formulate action saturation as an explicit constrained optimization objective (Lagrangian or penalty method).
direction_board.rationale: The paper gives clip099 diagnostic power but doesn't treat it as a training signal. The finding that longer training makes clip099 worse suggests the optimization landscape has a basin of attraction toward saturation. Explicitly penalizing saturation could prevent this.

minimum_viable_experiment.setup: Add an explicit saturation penalty to PICA: clip_loss = λ * mean(max(0, |a_i| - 0.99)) for all action dimensions. Sweep λ ∈ {0, 0.01, 0.1, 1.0}. Compare to PICA without clip loss at ×4.
minimum_viable_experiment.expected_outcome: With appropriate λ, clip099 should decrease and ×4 success should increase. If the trade-off is real, there should be a Pareto frontier. If clip099 reduction doesn't help success, saturation is a symptom not a cause.

key_unaddressed_questions: Does PICA transfer to real hardware?
How does PICA compare with the same architecture + tactile feedback?
Is robustness to damping representative of robustness to real contact-load variations?
Can PICA handle multi-dimensional dynamics shifts simultaneously?
Does the floating-hand assumption limit real-world applicability?
analysis_notes.most_direct_next_work: Sim-to-real transfer + force feedback comparison (directions 1-2) — these directly validate the paper's core claims in real-world settings.
analysis_notes.highest_risk_direction: Whole-body humanoid loco-manipulation (direction 4) — high complexity, many confounding factors, but highest impact if successful.
analysis_notes.lowest_effort_high_impact: Make clip099 a loss term (direction 7) — can be done in the same codebase with minimal changes, addresses a finding the paper itself identifies.
